Tafseer:
The importance of remembering Allah:
We have explained in various places that the primary aspect of 'tasbih' is purification (tanzeeh)—namely, declaring Allah Almighty pure and elevated above everything that is contrary to His high status. The correct concept of Allah Almighty is the source of all knowledge, gnosis, strength, and confidence. If any flaw occurs in it, man departs from the highway of correct gnosis and falls into the hands of Satan, which ultimately deprives his heart of the blessing of faith and trust, the light of peace of mind, and the strength of resolve and firmness. The correct gnosis of Allah and His remembrance keep the heart stable, straight, and satisfied: (Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured) (Ar-Ra'd, 13:28).
The highest and standard form of 'tasbih', as we repeatedly explain, is prayer—especially the night prayer (Tahajjud)—but just as breathing is necessary for physical life at all times, remembering Allah is necessary for spiritual life at all times. For this reason, not only during the times of prayers but also during other activities of life, the heart should remain populated with the remembrance of Allah Almighty so that Satan finds no opportunity to overcome it. We explained this under Surah Al-Muzzammil, verse 7: (Indeed, for you by day is prolonged occupation). Here, this instruction is given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to attain patience and firmness; therefore, it is used in its comprehensive sense.

Every work of Allah has a gradual progress based on His wisdom:
In this and the subsequent verses, attention is drawn to several attributes of the Exalted God, clarifying the reality that every work of His is subject to a gradual progress based entirely on His wisdom. Therefore, the servant must continue executing His commands with patience and firmness, hoping that the final destination of the path He has commanded him to walk will eventually manifest with all its blessings. The delay will be based on His wisdom, and the difficulties encountered will contain benefits for both this world and the Hereafter.
Khalq (creation): 'Khalaqa' literally means to create, but it is also used for preparing the basic form or mold of something, as in:
(Indeed, I create [akhluqu] for you from clay like the form of a bird, then I breathe into it and it becomes a bird by permission of Allah) (Al-Imran, 3:49).
Here, it is used in this sense.
Taswiyah (proportioning): 'Taswiyah' means to make something balanced, level, and proportioned. Here, context indicates it is used in this final sense. The meaning is: if man reflects on his own creation, he will see that Allah Almighty did not create him all at once; rather, his drop had to pass through many stages to become a gem. One phase is when its basic form is prepared, then slowly His power shapes its features.

The wide meaning of 'And Who destined and [then] guided' (wa-lladhi qaddara fa-hada):
This is also the case with the development of his capabilities and his physical and intellectual maturity. Nature has placed within him the inclinations for the things his life depends on—for example, having made the child dependent on milk, He provided milk in the mother's breast, and then inspired the child to suckle.
In subsequent stages, when his needs expand, the reserves are protected in the earth. And the Creator gave man intellect to discover these reserves, open the ways to acquire them, and invent methods to benefit from them.
Similarly, for his spiritual and moral development, Allah Almighty endowed him with the awareness of good and evil, and sent His prophets and messengers to teach and guide: which path of life is in accordance with his nature and leads to his success, and which path is contrary to his nature and leads to his ruin.
From birth to death, all stages, stations, periods, trials, and passes of life are destined by Allah Almighty, and they are bound to happen. Then, He has also shown the way to fulfill them. If a person chooses that way, his ship of life passes safely through every storm; and if he deviates to follow his desires, this becomes the cause of his ruin. The words 'And Who destined and [then] guided' encompass all these meanings, the details of which are lengthy. We explained this under Surah Ta-Ha, verse 50: (He said, 'Our Lord is He Who gave each thing its form and then guided').

Answering a literary difficulty:
This portion contains a literary difficulty that must be understood first to clarify its context.
'Ghuthā'an ahwa' has been generally translated as dark chaff or black stubble, but in Arabic, while the word 'ghuthā'' indeed refers to foam and stubble, 'ahwa' is never used for the blackness that appears in something due to old age, decay, and palling. Rather, it is used for the dark green or reddish-black color that appears in something due to its freshness, lushness, fertility, and growth. It is used repeatedly as an attribute of vegetation and gardens to show the intensity of their greenness and density. From this, it was used metaphorically for a healthy, robust, and handsome youth. This is because those in excellent health with abundant blood possess a dark red tint on their lips. The famous pre-Islamic poet, Ta'abbata Sharran, wrote:
'He moves among the tribe as a well-dressed, handsome youth [ahwa],
and when he fights, he is a lion of the forest.'
Although 'ghuthā'' is used for the foam of butter and the stubble of a flood, its usage is also well known for the vegetation that has become thick and dark green due to the fertility of the earth. Our teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) has cited several classical verses in support of this. For the sake of brevity, we present only a verse by Qatami, describing a valley:
'They camped in a lush green valley whose middle was filled with thick, dark green vegetation [dhee ghuthā'] hanging over its sides.'
Since the attribute of 'ghuthā'' in the verse under discussion is 'ahwa', it is used in this second meaning; otherwise, a mismatch would occur between the attribute and the noun, as 'ahwa'—as noted—is never used for the blackness of decay. There is no precedent for this in classical Arabic. Furthermore, the context of the discourse also refuses that meaning.
Thus, the correct meaning of 'And Who brings out the pasture and then makes it dark green [ghuthā'an ahwa]' is: glorify the Lord Who causes vegetation to grow from the earth like delicate needles, and then makes it thick and dark green.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 4 and 5 was combined in the original text.]

The reality explained to the Prophet: This is the primary objective for which the preceding introduction was explained—namely: the divine law of gradual progress manifest in human creation, in proportioning and executing capabilities, and in growing and developing vegetation, is the same law under which Allah Almighty will deal with you. The stages of the mission He has commissioned you with and the revelation He has blessed you with will be covered gradually, and soon you will see that the obstacles on the path have been covered and the final destination has arrived.
A specific instruction to the Prophet regarding revelation: 'We will make you recite, [O Muhammad], and you will not forget' (sanuqri'uka fa-la tansa) - This verse appears in the exact place and for the same purpose as Surah Ta-Ha, verse 114:
(And do not hasten with the Qur'an before its revelation is completed to you),
and Surah Al-Qiyamah, verse 16:
(Move not your tongue with it to hasten [your recitation of] it).
We explained in detail under those verses that during this period of opposition, the Prophet's only support was the divine help descending as revelation. It was the revelation that provided him with the provision of strength and resolve, guided him, and answered the opponents' objections. For these reasons, he constantly awaited the revelation, and when it descended, he displayed the eagerness and anxiety that a hungry child displays when embraced by his mother. In his eagerness, he wanted the entire revelation to enter his chest in a single breath; and since it was a great trust, he would repeat every word with his tongue, lest any word escape his memory.
Upon his anxiety, eagerness, and haste, Allah Almighty reassured him: 'Do not hasten with this revelation. It will descend in accordance with the specified program We have appointed, and in this lies wisdom.'
Alongside this, he was also reassured regarding its preservation: 'Allah Almighty will arrange its preservation in such a manner that you will not forget a single word of it.' In Surah Al-Qiyamah, this is expressed as:
(Move not your tongue with it to hasten [your recitation of] it. Indeed, upon Us is its collection and its recitation. So when We have recited it, then follow its recitation. Then indeed, upon Us is its clarification.) (Al-Qiyamah, 75:16-19).

'Except what Allah should will' (illa ma sha'a-llah) - That is: excepted from this are only those commands that were temporary. Upon the completion of their term, Allah Almighty Himself informed of their completion. This points to the temporary commands that were later abrogated.
Referencing His comprehensive knowledge to teach true trust: 'Indeed, He knows what is declared and what is hidden' (innahu ya'lamu-l-jahra wa ma yakhfa) - That is: do not assume that your Lord is unaware of the situations and difficulties you face. He is not unaware, but knows both what is declared and what is hidden. The meaning is: your anxieties and prayers are in His knowledge, and the open hostilities and secret conspiracies of the enemies are also in His knowledge; so since everything is in His knowledge, and He possesses all power and authority, be assured that you will not be deprived of the help and guidance you need. This is expressed in Surah Ta-Ha as:
(To Him belongs what is in the heavens and what is on the earth and what is between them and what is under the soil. And if you speak aloud—then indeed, He knows the secret and what is hidden.) (Ta-Ha, 20:6-7).
Note that true trust and reliance on Allah Almighty are produced by keeping His comprehensive knowledge and all-encompassing power in mind.

Glad tiding of the destination of ease:
This is an explicit glad tiding to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): these difficult days will not continue, but We will soon guide you to a path of ease. 'Yusra' (ease) is an attribute whose noun 'tariqah' (path) is omitted based on context. The meaning of 'taysir' (easing) was explained—showing that it refers to preparing something for a noble purpose. This indicates: the difficulties you face today are for your training, so that Allah Almighty makes you worthy of the favors He will bless you with in the future. This is the requirement of His law referenced in: 'And Who destined and [then] guided' and: 'And Who brings out the pasture and then makes it dark green'—showing that the obstacles on the path today are for trial and training under His law. The path ahead is clear; rely on His guidance. The destination of ease (yusr) comes after difficulty ('usr).

The divine law regarding guidance and deviation:
That is: do not be disheartened or disappointed by their opposition and disregard. Placing guidance in their hearts is not your responsibility, such that you should exhaust yourself over them. Your duty is only to remind. When you see they are ready to listen, remind them; otherwise, leave them to their destiny.

This explains who will listen to your call, and who will turn away. It is stated: those who possess some fear of God and the Hereafter in their hearts will listen, and those whose hearts are devoid of this fear will remain unaffected. The meaning is: those who are turning away from your call are doing so because of the disease in their own hearts, as they regard this worldly life as the only life. They have no fear of the Hereafter. Leave such unfortunate individuals to their state; they will be cast into the Great Fire prepared for them, where they will neither die nor live, but remain in eternal punishment. They will wish for death, but death will not come to relieve them.
Recall the divine law: those who preserve the light endowed within their nature find the Prophet's call to be the voice of their own heart. Even if this light is weak, they are awakened by the Prophet's reminder; but those in whom this light is completely extinguished are like the dead, whom nothing but the Trumpet can awaken.
What is stated here is explained in detail in the subsequent twin Surah—Al-Ghashiyah, where He states:
(So remind, [O Muhammad]; you are only a reminder. You are not over them a controller. Except him who turns away and disbelieves—then Allah will punish him with the greatest punishment. Indeed, to Us is their return. Then indeed, upon Us is their account.) (Al-Ghashiyah, 88:21-26).
Note that Al-Ghashiyah uses 'the greatest punishment' ('adhab-al-akbar), and this Surah uses 'the Great Fire' (an-nar-al-kubra). This same is stated in slightly different words in both twin Surahs, and will be explained in the next Surah.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 10, 11, 12, and 13 was combined in the original text.]

The reward of those whose hearts are alive:
The preceding verses described the end of those who turn away; this describes the reward of those who benefit from the reminder, pointed to by: 'He who fears will remember'. It is stated: indeed, success belongs to those who purified themselves by accepting the Prophet's reminder and keeping themselves free from the impurity of shirk and disbelief. The doors of success will open for them in this world and the Hereafter.

The status of prayer in religion:
'And mentions the name of his Lord and prays' (wa dhakaras-ma rabbihi fa-salla) - This is the primary sign of purification and its primary method. We noted that the source of all knowledge is indeed His attributes. It is through His attributes that our beliefs are clarified, and these beliefs impose rights and responsibilities on us regarding our Lord and His creation. Thus, prayer is mentioned here as the primary expression of faith in Allah. We repeatedly explain that prayer is the primary expression of faith, and the foundation and guardian of the entire Shariah; refer to Surah Al-Mu'minun for details.

The actual cause behind the opponents' hostility:
Finally, addressing the leaders of the Quraysh, this reveals the actual cause behind their hostility: your love for the world. You are not ready to accept the Hereafter and sacrifice your worldly interests for it; therefore, you fabricate baseless doubts to convince your people that your refusal is based on some arguments. But, O foolish ones, remember that in your love for this temporary life, you are losing an eternal kingdom! The Hereafter is better and more permanent; if you possess understanding, seek it!
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 16 and 17 was combined in the original text.]

The teachings of the Qur'an are the teachings of all prophets:
That is: what the Prophet is telling you is not something new or unique. All past prophets and messengers taught the same: the true life is the life of the Hereafter, and only a person's own deeds will benefit him there, and no one will bear the burden of another, as stated in Surah An-Najm:
(Or has he not been informed of what was in the scriptures of Moses, and of Abraham, who fulfilled [his obligations]? That no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another) (An-Najm, 53:36-38).
The scriptures of Moses and other prophets are preserved. Although they contain alterations, they still contain the teachings of monotheism and the Resurrection so clearly that reading them strengthens faith.
Abraham taught his progeny, and although it was not in a written form but oral instruction, the Children of Israel preserved it in writing in their scriptures, and their prophets repeatedly reminded them of it—testified to by their scriptures and referenced by the Qur'an.
The Children of Ishmael, being unlettered, did not preserve it in writing. They preserved it as oral traditions, but with the passage of time, it was lost behind innovations. However, with the mission of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), it was renewed and completed, serving as the foundation of the true religion until the Day of Resurrection.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 18 and 19 was combined in the original text.]
